holyfamily. edu A letter to the evaluation committee Modernizing Holy Family's web presence, together. Small College Consulting is honored to present this proposal to Holy Family University. In partnership with your internal team, our approach will strengthen the institution's website and its connected marketing channels. It will also position University personnel to manage and evolve the site in the years ahead with little external vendor support, and in most cases none. Members of the Small College Consulting team already hold working relationships with members of the University's leadership. We are eager to deepen them, and to extend the scope of our work together. What follows is our considered answer to every requirement the RFP sets out. This proposal answers the RFP in full, organized as requested into Sections A through G, each addressed in the pages that follow. In preparation for this engagement, Small College Consulting has developed proprietary technology that supports seamless redesign and content migration from the existing Drupal installation into WordPress, while simultaneously optimizing the new site for AI-engine visibility. It has been built, tested, and validated against real institutional content. Respectfully submitted, Scott Novak · Chris Coons · James Vineburgh · Eric Yerke Small College Consulting · Submitted 17 April 2026 The thesis, in three numbers Enrolling more of the right students, faster. Every decision in this proposal answers one question: how does the redesign help Holy Family enroll more students? Not a modern website for its own sake, but a website that measurably moves inquiries, applications, and confirmed enrollments. Kickoff → Launch Jun → Sep '26 Three months to build. Ninety days of stabilization through November. Live for the Fall 2026 recruitment cycle, rather than the Spring 2027 window implied by the RFP's outer timeline. Investment $145,000 All-in base scope. Slate integration included. Optional add-ons priced separately per RFP Section E. Soft-exit at any approval gate with full IP retained. Evidence 2,300 + leads James Vineburgh's admissions concierge at a peer Catholic university has been live since early 2025, grounded in the institution's own content, with no campaign spend. The same architecture ships here. Day 1 Value Pack · before you even sign Six artifacts, drafted inside this response. Training delivered. Ninety-day support begins. Four people on this engagement Small College Consulting. The four principals below are the same four who work with Rick Mitchell's team week one and at launch. No substitution after contract signing. SN Partner · Strategic Lead Scott Novak 30+ years in small-college enrollment. Founder of Underscore, acquired by Carnegie in 2021. 300+ institutions advised. CC Partner · Enrollment Chris Coons Prior chief enrollment officer at two small colleges. Ensures every design & content decision traces to RFI, application, yield. JV Engagement Lead · AI Architect James Vineburgh Built the Carnegie admissions concierge ( 2,300+ unattended leads since 2025). Day-to-day client lead. EY Lead Designer · AI Developer Eric Yerke Multi-thousand-page CMS migrations and design-system work. Owns the component library , migration tooling, and WCAG pipeline through day 90. After launch · RFP Section F Post-Launch Support A website in 2026 is not a deliverable. Launch is the opening of a longer conversation. With WordPress, with WCAG, and with a generation of AI systems that will keep releasing through 2027 and beyond. This section describes the care the site will need, what sits inside the ninety-day stabilization included in base scope, and what Small College Consulting stewards afterward so the Fall 2026 gains hold through Fall 2029. I. Drift 01 · AI citation Four models. New releases every few weeks. ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and Perplexity. The four systems the RFP names. Each ship meaningful updates on the order of weeks. The llms. txt file, schema markup, and canonical facts delivered at launch are not one-time installs; they need to travel with the models. Our recommendation: a monthly spot-check against a fixed prompt set, with any corrections pushed back into the site's canonical sources rather than patched into outside copy. Answers RFP Section 6 · Monitoring AI system accuracy in representing HFU content II. Drift 02 · WordPress & plugin cadence Core releases, plugin updates, predictable cycles. WordPress's public release history runs roughly one major core release every four months, with security patches in between. An enterprise site typically carries twenty to thirty plugins, each on its own schedule. Updates are staged through Pantheon's dev → test → live environments, with compatibility testing before production. The RFP's request for "plug-in governance and update cadence" is a recurring commitment, not a checkbox at launch. Answers RFP Section 6 · CMS Requirements & Security Standards III. Drift 03 · Accessibility & performance Launch-day conformance is a snapshot. WCAG 2.2 AA conformance on September 1, 2026 is a signed statement about the site on that date. Every new program page, uploaded image without alt text, and added plugin is a chance for the score to slip. Our recommendation: automated scans quarterly, one manual review annually, paired with Core Web Vitals monitoring and schema validation. The intent is to catch issues before complaints, not after them. Answers RFP Section 4 Goal 5 · Section 6 Performance & Accessibility What Holy Family's team owns after day 90. Content velocity. New program pages, news and events, faculty profile edits, image replacements. Sits with Rick Mitchell's team from the first week after launch. Phase 6 training is built around this handoff. Campaign pages that once took months ship in days, because the templates and component library do the structural work and your writers do the writing. What Small College Consulting stewards, if you elect to continue. AI citation monitoring, WordPress core and plugin update cycles, quarterly WCAG audits, Slate integration health checks, schema validation, and the llms. txt freshness record. The Extended Support line in the budget ($3,500 per month) is scoped to these specific responsibilities, not to generic hours against a retainer. If Holy Family chooses not to continue past day 90, the documentation and recorded training leave your team positioned to absorb the work internally. A site that silently decays over two years sends fewer qualified inquiries to Slate each quarter, drops in AI citations, and surrenders the organic-search ground the launch earned. Stewardship, framed this way, is not a cost center. It is the input that keeps the Fall 2026 enrollment gains in place for Fall 2027, 2028, and 2029. Budget · RFP Section E One fixed fee. No scope creep. The base scope is priced at $145,000, all-in, including Slate integration. Optional add-ons are priced separately so the committee can scope each independently. Fully expanded comparative analysis with visual diffs. Architecture Sitemap atlas Every section, nested. Every URL, mapped. Every 301, drafted. Design · system Design system Tokens, components, patterns. The library your team inherits at launch. In closing A modern website. One that your team can own. We are honored to submit this proposal. The site we will build together is a website Rick Mitchell's team can run, Katherine Primus's team can measure, and Dr. Anne Prisco's cabinet can point to as evidence that the institution's digital presence matches its educational rigor. Early June 2026. Live by September 1. Ninety days of stabilization through November. $145,000, all-in. Soft-exit at every gate. We hope to begin. helps Holy Family enroll. Each earns its place by answering one question: does this help Holy Family enroll more of the right students? None are experimental. Each has a working analogue in production today. 3 included with the base 3 optional, priced separately 1 ongoing, quarterly The enrollment journey How a curious student becomes an applicant. Five stages every prospective student moves through. And where AI does quiet work at each one, on behalf of Holy Family's team. 1 Curious A student asks ChatGPT or Google about nursing programs near Philadelphia. AI on HFU's side Every program page is written in a way AI assistants can read and cite. 2 Finds Holy Family The answer comes back with Holy Family named, and links straight to holyfamily. edu. AI on HFU's side Holy Family shows up in the answer. Not a third-party ranking page. 3 Explores They land on a page built for someone like them. A working nurse, a transfer parent, an adult returner. AI on HFU's side Marketing ships tailored landing pages in days, not months. 4 Asks They have a question at 11 pm. About aid, transfer credits, clinical hours. And get a real answer. AI on HFU's side A concierge answers in Holy Family's voice when counselors are home. 5 Applies They request information, meet a counselor by name, and begin the application. AI on HFU's side A qualified lead lands in Slate with the conversation attached. Every piece of AI on this page shortens the distance between stage 1 and stage 5. Try a mission Put the AI to work. Pick a mission. Each button opens the concierge in the corner and asks it to do one real enrollment task on Holy Family's behalf. No setup, no sign-in. Just click and watch. Capture Capture a prospective student as a lead. Watch the concierge run the conversation a counselor would run, and hand back what Slate would receive. Try it Draft Draft a landing page for working-parent nurses. See what the AI page-generation workflow returns before a human touches it. Try it See See what AI assistants say about Holy Family. Read a model answer and the facts that need to be pinned down for it to be accurate. Try it Propose Propose this week's three blog posts. The Monday memo the marketing director gets. Three drafts with the reasoning behind them. Try it Counsel Counsel a transfer student with 48 credits. The adult-learner concierge running the conversation that turns interest into an inquiry. Try it Brief Brief the marketing lead on this quarter. A one-page enrollment intelligence memo. What's working, what's fading, what to ship next. Try it I · Included with the base Idea 01 / AI discoverability & SEO Holy Family's site answers when a family asks AI about Holy Family. The site ships with a canonical llms. txt file, schema. org markup on every program page ( CollegeOrUniversity , EducationalOccupationalProgram , Course , Offer , FAQPage , BreadcrumbList ), a tuned robots. txt that welcomes GPTBot, ClaudeBot, PerplexityBot, and Google-Extended , and a citation baseline measured in July 2026 with 30- and 90-day re-checks. We verify Holy Family's Google Knowledge Panel and submit structured answers for the questions prospective families actually ask. llms. txt Canonical program list, tuition, deadlines, accreditation. One file every assistant reads. Schema Six schema. org types wired per page. Programs exposed as machine-readable offers. Crawler policy robots. txt tuned for GPTBot, ClaudeBot, PerplexityBot, Google-Extended. Baseline Ten standard questions across four assistants, July 2026. Re-checked at 30 and 90 days. Knowledge panel Verified Google claim, People Also Ask answers structured and submitted. Citation share Tracked quarterly: when an assistant answers about HFU, does it link back or not? How it
helps enroll When a family asks ChatGPT "is Holy Family a good nursing school?", the answer cites holyfamily. edu directly. Not a third-party ranking site whose copy you don't control. Approve Edit Reject II · Optional, priced separately Idea 04 · 24/7 Admissions Chatbot A counselor who's awake when the family is. Admissions · holyfamily. edu · Tuesday 9:47 pm My daughter is finishing her associate's in nursing this May. Can she transfer to Holy Family for her BSN? Yes. Holy Family's RN-to-BSN track is built for exactly this. Most students transfer in around 60 credits and finish the BSN in 15 months, online, with three start dates a year.
Cited · /academics/rn-to-bsn What would it cost after aid? She has her federal loans maxed already. For a working RN with federal limits reached, Pennsylvania PHEAA grants and Holy Family institutional aid often bring the effective per-credit cost into the $400. 500 range , depending on FAFSA and income. To model your daughter's exact numbers, I can book you with Maria Alvarez, our graduate nursing counselor. She has Tuesday 8:15 pm or Thursday 11:00 am open.

helps enroll The reference build has generated more than 2,300 inbound leads since early 2025 at a peer Catholic institution, with no campaign spend. Cited · /academics/rn-to-bsn Idea 05 / Conversational site search Search that understands intent. A site search that embeds every program page, blog post, and the full financial-aid library into a vector index, classifies query intent (info / price / apply / compare), and returns a direct answer with cited sources. Not a list of keyword matches. Every query is logged. The top 50 questions each week become marketing's standing content brief for the autoblogging engine. Vector index 1,200+ pages embedded; re-indexed on every publish. Intent classifier Routes query to info, price, apply, or compare. Each with its own response shape. Cited answers Every response links to the source page. No unsourced claims. Weekly brief Top 50 queries delivered to marketing Monday morning. Feeds the content engine. How it
helps enroll "What nursing program fits around a full-time job" returns a direct answer instead of twelve keyword matches. Prospects find the right page on the first try, and marketing sees every unanswered question. The pages write themselves because prospects asked for them in their own words. Optional · priced separately Vector / RAG Intent > keywords Idea 06 · Adult-learner pathway For the student at 9:47 pm, after the kids are asleep. Then we started drafting. Before we wrote a page of this proposal, we crawled all 1,601 URLs on holyfamily. edu, read the RFP four times, pulled every Niche and Glassdoor review we could find, captured the live site on desktop and mobile, and drafted the first production deliverable. An llms. txt file that would make Holy Family discoverable to ChatGPT, Claude, and Perplexity the day it ships. What follows is what we found, what it unlocks for enrollment, and where the work shows up in the engagement. 1,601 URLs crawled from the live sitemap. xml on Apr 11, 2026 7 findings. Each one a specific enrollment opportunity 6 pages screenshotted at desktop and mobile as evidence 1 deliverable already drafted before kickoff Section  01 · What’s already working Holy Family’s web operation is further along than you might assume. The discovery brief starts here on purpose. Before we describe gaps, we want to name what the marketing and IT teams have already built. Because the redesign inherits it, and because the committee should hear us say it out loud. 01 A mature analytics stack is already live. The current site runs Google Tag Manager ( GTM-5FPBQK3 ) with GA4, Hotjar, and Crazy Egg attached, and SiteImprove for accessibility monitoring. That is a serious instrumentation set. Most universities we audit are missing two or three of those tools. We don’t have to sell you on measurement; we have to help you act on it. Observed in · holyfamily. edu · Apr 11, 2026 02 Content is being published, and recently. Of the 1,601 URLs in the sitemap, 670 are news articles. Roughly 42% of the site. The most recent update lands March 17, 2026 , six weeks before the RFP was issued. This is a team that writes. The redesign’s job is to make that work easier to ship, not to replace the muscle that already exists. sitemap. xml · 670 news URLs · updated Mar 17, 2026 03 The strategic work is done. Section 3 of the RFP lists the prep work HFU completed in 2025: content audit, governance guidelines, peer competitive analysis, stakeholder surveys, preliminary site-structure recommendations. That homework is a head start, not a placeholder. Our discovery phase will build on those findings, not repeat them. Holy Family Website Redesign RFP · March 2026 · Section 3 “Outdated design that doesn’t reflect our vibrant campus culture. Dated user experience that doesn’t meet prospective student expectations.”
Holy Family Website Redesign RFP · March 2026 · Section 1 “Current Challenges” That is Holy Family’s own sentence about Holy Family’s own website. Every finding that follows is a specific, evidenced version of that sentence. And an answer to the question underneath it: how does fixing this help us enroll more of the right students, faster? Section  02 · Seven findings from the audit Each one is an enrollment argument first, a design argument second. We scored the live site against the RFP’s stated goals, then against what a 27-year-old adult learner deciding between Holy Family and La Salle actually experiences on a phone at 9:47 pm. Where the two views diverged, we wrote a finding. Each one is one claim, the evidence that supports it, and the opportunity it represents for the Fall 2026 recruiting class. I. The vibrancy gap The real Holy Family is warmer than the website admits. Above the fold today: a single stock-feeling lab photo, yellow pill buttons, and the headline “What Will You Study?”. A question form-factor, not an invitation. Below the fold: ranking badges in a navy slab, a news grid, and a centered stat row. What the homepage does not show: a named student, a faculty member by name, a dated story about a classroom moment, or a sentence in a human voice. That gap is not a failure of the marketing team. It is a constraint of the current CMS and template. The warmth exists; it is living in 670 news posts three clicks deep. The job of the redesign is to bring that voice to the surface. holyfamily. edu · homepage · desktop 1440 × 900 Captured Apr 15, 2026 “From the beginning, I felt a strong sense of community… The professors are always looking out for our best interest. They do fake interviews with us, take our professional pictures, help with our resume, and have career fair events every single month.” Current student · Niche. com reviews · accessed April 2026 What this unlocks A homepage and a program-page template that let HFU’s real voices. Named students, named faculty, dated moments. Run above the fold. That is an enrollment argument: prospective students who see a specific human story on the hero convert at measurably higher rates than those shown institutional photography. II. Adult learner unserved The RFP names adult and graduate enrollment as the growth priority. The site is built for 17-year-olds. RFP, Primary Goal #2: “Design with prospective student experience as top priority, with specific focus on improving enrollment in Adult and Graduate programs.” Now look at what a 34-year-old working nurse actually sees when she lands on the grad-nursing page on her phone at 9:47 pm. /grad-nursing , / , and a routed 404 · iPhone 14 Pro viewport (390 × 844) Captured Apr 15, 2026 No tuition number above the fold. No time-to-degree. No schedule (evening? online? hybrid?). No outcomes. The floating accessibility widget overlaps the “Request Information” button. The one CTA on the screen. And a mistyped URL sends her to a blue 404 with a pink “Journey of Support” block that does not name her program. She is gone. What she needs in 10 seconds Surfaced on current /grad-nursing? Total tuition, or tuition per credit No Time to degree (months or semesters) No Evening / online / hybrid format No Start dates & application deadline No Outcomes (employment, licensure, salary) No A visible Request-Information button Partially * Observed on /grad-nursing, mobile viewport, Apr 15, 2026 * Obscured by the accessibility overlay What this unlocks A dedicated adult-learner flow with cost, schedule, outcomes, and one obvious next step above the fold on mobile. This is the single finding the RFP’s Primary Goal #2 was written to fix. And the pillar of the engagement where Enrollment Intelligence pays for itself. III. Sitemap weight The sitemap is carrying a decade of accumulated weight. 1,601 URLs across more than 180 distinct top-level prefixes. For a university this size the healthy range is 8. 15 prefixes and 500. 800 pages. The RFP already knows this: “Streamline content to reduce redundancy.” The crawl tells us where the weight actually sits. Section Pages Observation /about (news, leadership, policies) 917 Largest bucket by far; ~670 are news archive, many pre-2024 /academics (programs & courses) 253 Duplicated request-info forms across majors; no shared template /student-experience 54 Housing, orientation, activities. Healthy size /admissions-aid 41 23 distinct RFI forms, one per program office Test, legacy, holiday pages ~25 /041425-it-test-page-chatbot , /Season-Greetings , /shore_reception2025 Full site crawl · sitemap. xml · Apr 11, 2026 Target at launch: ~650 URLs (−60%) “Streamline” does not mean delete. It means archive the 2021 press releases, consolidate the 23 request-info forms into one routed engine, and retire the test pages. The redesigned site ships with roughly 650 live URLs, a shared program-page template, and a content-lifecycle policy the marketing team can run themselves. What this unlocks A marketing team that can find, edit, and archive content in seconds instead of minutes. A crawl budget spent on 650 pages that earn it, not 950 that don’t. A new page ships in days. The pace the Fall 2026 recruitment push actually needs. IV. Accessibility exposure The ADA Title II deadline was April 24, 2026. It has already passed. The Department of Justice final rule that extended WCAG 2.1 AA compliance to public-facing higher-education sites took effect nine days after this proposal is due. SiteImprove is already in HFU’s stack, which means the measurement is happening; whether the fixes have landed is a separate question. The most commonly failed WCAG items on university sites. Alt text, color contrast in branded palettes, keyboard-navigable forms, inaccessible PDFs. Are the items most expensive to retrofit on an existing Drupal theme and cheapest to build into a new design system from day one. A ground-up rebuild to WCAG 2.2 AA is the cleanest remediation path; a retrofit is measurably not. “All digital content, including websites, web applications, online learning platforms, and third-party tools, must meet WCAG 2.1 Level AA standards… Public universities (Title II entities) with populations over 50,000 must comply by April 24, 2026.” U. S. Department of Justice · ADA Title II Final Rule · April 2024 What this unlocks A new site that is compliant on day one, documented on day one, and defensible on day one. Not a retrofitted site with an open punch-list the legal office has to explain. Accessibility, done correctly, is also an enrollment argument: it expands the candidate pool. V. AI-invisible AI assistants can’t find a canonical answer about Holy Family. We requested holyfamily. edu/llms. txt on April 15, 2026. The server returned 404. The standard response for a file that does not exist. Nothing on the site tells ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Perplexity which pages contain the authoritative answers about tuition, programs, outcomes, or deadlines. When a prospective student asks an AI assistant about Catholic universities in Philadelphia with a strong nursing program, the assistant is left to guess. The RFP’s AI-Optimized Content Architecture section names this directly: llms. txt , Schema. org EducationalOrganization / CollegeOrUniversity / Course , JSON-LD, FAQ formatting, canonical sources. Rick Mitchell is already thinking about this. This finding is validation, not discovery. Signal Present on live site /llms. txt 404 · absent Schema. org JSON-LD on program pages None observed FAQ-pattern content for retrieval Sparse Recency signals on key pages Partial Observed on holyfamily. edu · Apr 15, 2026 AI-referred visitors convert at ~4.4× organic (Profound, 2025) What this unlocks Holy Family shows up inside the conversation prospective students are already having with AI assistants. With the school’s own words, on the school’s own terms. See the next section: we have already drafted the file. VI. CMS / team mismatch Drupal is a great CMS for a five-engineer team. HFU has one. The current theme lives at /themes/custom/hfu/ , confirming a custom Drupal build. The internal team, per the RFP and the Apr 14 strategy call, is a Director of Digital Experience plus a web strategist. That is a maintainer-shaped CMS attached to a managing-shaped team. The mismatch is the actual problem. Not Drupal itself. The RFP is explicit: “We have a preference for WordPress.” They want out. The work of the engagement is not to debate CMSes; it is to execute the migration cleanly, train the team, and leave behind a component library, a Gutenberg block kit, and a Figma system a two-person team can actually run. The RFP tells us that independence is the real goal: “Enable easy content management for non-technical users.” What this unlocks A marketing team that ships a campaign landing page in days, not quarters. A migration budget that stops compounding with every Drupal point release. A university that will not need to pay an agency to rebuild this site from scratch again. VII. Best stories, buried Holy Family’s best numbers are nowhere above the fold. We read every program page on the nursing track and every outcome page under /about. The story Holy Family is actually in a position to tell is extraordinary: 100% NCLEX first-time pass rate in nursing. ~93% employed within six months of graduation. 82. 83% first-year retention , top 30% of U. S. institutions. Tuition $36,388. Below the national average of $47,097. Roughly 100% of undergraduates receive some form of financial aid. None of those numbers are the first thing a 9:47 pm visitor sees. They are reachable only by a determined researcher. The committee already knows this; Niche reviewers know this; Glassdoor reviewers know this. The website is the one place HFU controls the narrative. And right now it is the least-developed voice in the conversation. “The website is the one place HFU can tell its own story at scale… Acknowledge the reviews exist, then show that the website is how HFU gets to tell the fuller, truer story. In its own voice, in a place prospective students look before they look at Niche.” From our own discovery brief · pain-points finding 06 · April 2026 What this unlocks A homepage, a nursing landing page, and an adult-learner flow that lead with the outcomes HFU has earned. Cited, sourced, and above the fold. That is how the fuller story gets told at scale, by the university, on the university’s own site. Section  03 · We’ve already started The first deliverable of the engagement is already drafted. Most RFP responses describe work that begins on kickoff day. We think the committee deserves a working sample before that. So we wrote one. Below is the first production version of holyfamily. edu/llms. txt. Four personas account for almost all of HFU's recruitment mix. The first-year, the transfer, the adult learner, and the graduate or professional student. Each one arrives on the site at a different hour, on a different device, from a different channel, carrying a different question. The redesigned site is built to recognize which one just arrived, serve the answer they came for, and quietly feed that signal into Slate so the admissions team knows who to call first. That is what "enrollment intelligence" means in practice. Maria's storyboard is the sharpest version of the problem, but the same four decisions hurt Jasmine, Marcus, and Elena too. A slow mobile load is a lost first-year. A hidden price is a lost transfer. A broken RFI form is a lost graduate applicant. Fixing these four things at once lifts all four personas in parallel. 01 It optimizes for one persona and ignores the other three. The current holyfamily. edu was built for a traditional 17-year-old prospect arriving in daylight with a parent. That's Jasmine. And even for her, it underperforms. For Marcus, Maria, and Elena it's actively hostile: no transfer-credit calculator, no RN-to-BSN track, no graduate program filter. Every pixel assumes the visitor is on a campus tour; three out of four arrivals are not. The fix Dedicated paths in the IA for each of the four personas. Each tuned to the hour, device, and question they arrive with. 02 It hides the price. Program pages never show tuition. Not per credit, not per term, not total. The only path to a number is a form fill. A classic "gate the cost" pattern from 2014 enrollment marketing playbooks. Maria and Elena won't fill out four forms in one night; they'll drop HFU from the list. Jasmine's mom won't forward a link her family can't price. The fix Price and time-to-degree above the fold on every program page. No exceptions. 03 The RFI form breaks on a phone. The current Request Info form is a 12-field, three-column layout written for a desktop monitor and never reflowed for mobile. At 390px, fields overlap, the submit button hides behind the iOS keyboard, and validation errors scroll off-screen. That's 71% of HFU's sessions hitting a broken form. It is the single highest-leverage fix on the site. The fix One smart form. Four fields on mobile. Pre-filled from URL parameters. Slate-wired with a persona tag. 04 There's no path for the impatient. Every program page has exactly one conversion action. A "Request Information" button sitting five scrolls below the hero. Maria has 30 minutes. Marcus has a lunch break. Elena is between meetings. None of them want to start a conversation; they want to browse, then apply. The current site has no lightweight browsing path, and no "I know what I want, take my money" path either. The fix Three above-the-fold CTAs per program: Get Info · See Cost · Apply Now. How the site learns who's who The redesigned site recognizes each of the four personas before they've typed a single character. Persona classification isn't a survey. It's a quiet inference made from the signals every visitor already sends. The referral they came from, the page they landed on, the time of day, the device, the scroll depth, the questions they ask the concierge. The site pairs those signals with a small, transparent model that assigns a best-guess persona and tailors what the next screen emphasizes. Nothing personally identifying is stored without consent; the classification travels with the lead into Slate so advisors know which persona to treat them as. 01 · The signals What the first few seconds tell us. By the time a visitor has been on holyfamily. edu for thirty seconds , the site has a dozen signals that line up cleanly with persona. None of them are secret, invasive, or dependent on third-party cookies. They are signals the site already collects and then throws away. Referral & UTM "RN-to-BSN Philadelphia" paid search → almost certainly Maria. A LinkedIn ad → Elena. A TikTok referral → Jasmine. Hour of day & device Mobile after 8 pm skews adult learner. Laptop at noon skews graduate. Saturday morning skews first-year with a parent. Landing page & scroll Transfer-credit page, rapid scroll, direct jump to tuition → Marcus. Program browsing + photo gallery → Jasmine. First concierge question "Do you accept CCP credits?" classifies in one turn. "How much is the BSN?" in one turn. The site asks. It doesn't guess forever. 02 · What the site does with it The inference changes the next screen , not just a dashboard. Classification is only useful if it shapes what the visitor sees next. Future Options Assessment 

Guiding Institutions Through Strategic Choices: Equipping Small Colleges and Acquiring Institutions to Make Informed, Mission-Driven Decisions About Their Future. 

The Challenge 

Small colleges across the country face unprecedented pressures: shrinking enrollments, rising costs, demographic shifts, and intensifying competition. Many institutions find themselves with limited financial runway, forcing presidents and boards to confront high-stakes decisions without the full picture. Too often, action comes late, and closure feels like the only option. 

For acquiring institutions, the risks are equally high. A poorly executed merger or acquisition can strain finances, dilute brand identity, or create mission misalignment. Both sides need clarity, structure, and discipline to make choices that strengthen, rather than weaken, long-term stability. 

Future Options Assessment 

The Future Options Assessment is a confidential, structured, expert-led process that provides a comprehensive, 360-degree view of potential pathways forward. The goal is not to push any single outcome, but to equip leadership with the analysis, insights, and perspective needed to make informed, strategic decisions. 

What the assessment includes: 

Merger, Partnership, and Strategic Alliance Exploration: SWOT analysis, cultural fit review, synergy valuation, and identification of potential partners. 

Financial Impact Modeling: Side-by-side comparisons of merger, acquisition, alliance, status quo, and even closure scenarios, including costs, synergies, and long-term ROI. 

Student-Centered Review: Evaluating how each pathway affects access, stability, and long-term student success. 

Operational Assessment & Triage: Immediate support in areas such as enrollment, marketing, finance, and HR to extend financial runway, combined with long-term integration planning. 

Mission Alignment: Ensuring any recommended pathway strengthens, rather than dilutes, the institution’s identity and values. 

Our Team & Tools 

The assessment is delivered by SCC’s deep bench of experts: former college presidents, CFOs, enrollment leaders, and specialists in legal and HR. To complement this expertise, we integrate advanced diagnostic tools such as Perspective Data Science’s proprietary dashboard, which provides unmatched visibility into institutional financial health and operational sustainability. 

Why SCC? 

Unlike firms that simply “broker” mergers, SCC brings a holistic, student-first approach. We don’t just explore mergers; we stabilize immediate challenges, evaluate multiple options, and ensure that your mission remains at the center. The result is a process that is pragmatic, data-driven, and respectful of the identity your institution has built. 

Confidentiality 

SCC recognizes the sensitivity of the information shared during the Future Options Assessment process. All materials, data, and discussions provided by the interested institution are treated as strictly confidential. We are committed to maintaining the privacy and integrity of institutional information throughout the engagement. SCC is fully prepared to enter into Non-Disclosure Agreements (NDAs) as needed to protect both parties and ensure a secure, trust-based collaboration. 

The Outcome 

The Future Options Assessment delivers: 
Clarity on every possible path forward and the strengths and risks of each. 
Data-informed insights to guide boardroom and leadership decisions. 
Confidence that leadership has fully evaluated cultural, financial, and mission alignment. 

SCC’s mission is simple: to help colleges pursue futures that are strategic, sustainable, and mission-driven. Whether that means merger, partnership, acquisition, or a renewed independent path, our role is to ensure leaders move forward with clarity and confidence.

# Executive Summary

## The enrollment argument, first

Holy Family University's next website is, above all else, an enrollment instrument. The committee's Primary Goal #2 asks the selected agency to "design with prospective student experience as top priority, with specific focus on improving enrollment in Adult and Graduate programs." This proposal is organized around that requirement. Every design decision, every technical choice, and every content recommendation traces back to a single test: will this help Holy Family turn more prospective students into applicants, and more applicants into enrolled students, starting with the Fall 2026 recruitment cycle?

The calendar is the clearest expression of that priority. The RFP allows a 6 to 12 month window from kickoff to launch, which places the outer bound in March or April 2027. A launch in that window would miss the full Fall 2026 recruitment cycle and reach applicants only after decisions for the Spring and Fall 2027 incoming classes had already been formed. We recommend instead a **September 1, 2026 launch**, three months from the June kickoff the RFP requires. A September launch puts the new site in front of prospective students, their parents, and the AI assistants they increasingly consult *before* the recruitment cycle opens, not after it closes. That is an enrollment-cycle gain rather than an enrollment-cycle wait.

## Investment and commitments

**Investment: $145,000, fixed fee, all-in, for the base project scope.** This includes Slate integration, accessibility compliance to WCAG 2.2 AA, the consolidated content migration (roughly 900 to 1,000 active pages from the current 1,601), training, documentation, and 90 days of post-launch support. Optional add-ons (website personalization, content writing, extended support, additional training, an AI concierge chatbot, and AI-enhanced site search) are priced separately in Section E per the RFP's Section E requirement. No hourly add-ons, no expense markups, no platform reseller fees inside the base number.

**Kickoff: Early June 2026**, as required by the RFP's Proposal Validity clause.

**Launch: September 1, 2026**, our recommendation, framed as a recruitment-cycle gain rather than the RFP's implied 2027 baseline.

**Soft-exit clause:** At any milestone, Holy Family may end the engagement, retain full ownership of all work product (designs, code, content audit, redirect map, documentation, and repository), and return to a traditional vendor with no penalty.

## Who is responding

This proposal is submitted by **Small College Consulting**, a higher-education consulting practice built for small private colleges and universities, led by co-principals Scott Novak and Chris Coons. Scott and Chris contribute enrollment strategy and small-college leadership experience drawn from more than 60 years of combined work inside small private institutions. James Vineburgh leads the engagement day-to-day; his prior admissions concierge build at Carnegie has generated more than 2,300 inbound admissions leads since 2025 with no active campaign support. Eric Yerke leads AI development, design-system work, and the Drupal-to-WordPress migration. No subcontractors are disclosed in Section A.6; all delivery is performed by the Small College Consulting team, and any specialist engaged during Discovery (for example, a Slate-certified integration specialist) is paid out of the fixed fee and disclosed to HFU in writing before work begins.

## The three pillars

The proposal is organized around three connected commitments. Each appears throughout Sections B, C, and G.

1. **Whole Person Thesis.** This is the strategic and brand argument. The site should reflect the formation Holy Family actually offers, academic and vocational and spiritual, so a prospective student and their family recognize the institution rather than simply receive a pitch.
2. **Enrollment Intelligence.** This is the conversion and adult-learner argument. Slate is integrated in real time from day one; program pages are built as the canonical answer to the questions prospective students are actually asking; the analytics framework maps conversion events to the RFP's Awareness, Consideration, Decision, and Conversion funnel stages and can attribute enrolled students back to the website touchpoints that delivered them.
3. **AI Migration Lab.** This is the technical argument. The Drupal-to-WordPress migration uses AI where it is reliable (content audit, HTML cleanup, meta description and alt text generation, semantic redirect mapping, post-migration QA) and human review where judgment matters. This is the principal reason the September 1 launch is feasible without compromising accessibility, performance, or SEO.

## A live digital companion to this proposal

This proposal is submitted as a single PDF per the RFP's Section 12 requirement. A live digital companion is available at https://holy-family-two.vercel.app/, including an embedded AI concierge that can answer the committee's questions about this proposal directly, and an in-line commenting system for HFU feedback. The digital experience also demonstrates the collaboration mechanism we propose for the engagement itself, an alternative to Figma handoffs and traditional project-management tooling. The live companion is an illustration of what HFU's prospective students would encounter on the redesigned site, and evidence that the AI concierge described in Section C is a working capability, not a promise.

A successful redesign does not treat these audiences as competing priorities. It treats them as a design constraint that shapes every architectural and content decision from day one. Our approach rests on three principles: **Enrollment is the organizing metric.** Every design decision, content structure, and technical implementation will be evaluated against a simple question: does this make it easier for a prospective student to find the right program, understand its value, and take the next step? This does not mean the site ignores current students, alumni, or faculty. It means the primary user flows -- program discovery, information requests, application starts -- receive the most rigorous design attention, the fastest load times, and the clearest calls to action. Secondary audiences are served through defined pathways that do not compete with enrollment conversion. **Content strategy precedes visual design.** Holy Family's current site contains 1,601 indexed URLs across 180+ distinct top-level sections -- evidence of organic growth without centralized governance. Applying a new visual layer to this structure would produce a better-looking version of the same navigational complexity. We will conduct a rigorous content audit and consolidation process before any wireframe is drawn, reducing the active site to approximately 1,200 well-structured pages organized under 8-12 primary sections. This is not a cost-cutting measure. It is the single most impactful thing we can do for user experience. **The site we build is the site your team runs.** Rick Mitchell's team will own and operate this website for the next five or more years. Every component we build, every content model we define, and every workflow we establish will be designed for maintainability by a lean internal team -- not for ongoing agency dependency. Our deliverables include a documented component library, content templates with guardrails, and a structured knowledge transfer program. When we leave, your team should be faster and more capable than before we arrived. --- ## B.2 Discovery & Strategy Methodology ### Stakeholder Engagement University websites fail when departments feel unheard and succeed when departments feel represented within a coherent structure. Our discovery process is designed to surface every stakeholder need early, synthesize competing priorities into a shared framework, and establish decision-making authority before design begins. **Phase 1: Institutional Listening (Weeks 1-2)** We will conduct structured interviews and workshops with the following groups: - **Executive sponsors** (VP for Marketing and Communications, VP for Enrollment Management, VP for Institutional Effectiveness) -- strategic priorities, enrollment targets, brand positioning, and success metrics
- **Academic leadership** (Deans of the four Schools) -- program differentiation needs, faculty content requirements, and recruitment messaging priorities
- **Enrollment and admissions teams** -- current funnel bottlenecks, Slate CRM workflow, form conversion data, and seasonal patterns
- **Student services** (financial aid, registrar, student life, campus ministry) -- the most common questions they answer that the website should handle
- **IT and digital team** (Rick Mitchell's team) -- current technical pain points, content management workflows, integration requirements, and the design system philosophy already in place
- **Student representatives** (2-3 current students, 2-3 prospective) -- actual navigation behavior, content gaps, and device usage patterns Each session follows a consistent protocol: a pre-session questionnaire to capture baseline priorities, a 60-90 minute facilitated session, and a written summary delivered within 48 hours for validation. This creates an auditable record of stakeholder input that prevents scope disputes later. **Phase 2: Synthesis and Prioritization (Week 3)** We will produce a Stakeholder Findings Report that maps every surfaced need against three criteria: enrollment impact, implementation feasibility, and audience reach. Needs that score high on all three dimensions become core requirements. Needs that score high on impact but low on feasibility become Phase 2 candidates. Needs that score low on impact become documented-but-deferred items. This framework gives every stakeholder visibility into why decisions were made, even when their specific request was deferred. ### User Research We will supplement stakeholder interviews with direct user evidence: - **Analytics deep-dive:** Holy Family already runs GA4, Google Tag Manager, Crazy Egg, and Hotjar -- a mature analytics stack for a university this size. We will analyze existing behavioral data (heatmaps, session recordings, funnel drop-off points, search queries) before generating any new research. This respects the investment Rick's team has already made and accelerates our ramp-up.
- **Task-based usability testing:** 8-10 participants across three audience segments (prospective undergraduate, prospective graduate, current student) will attempt core tasks on the current site. We are testing navigation paths and content findability, not visual preferences.
- **Search query analysis:** Internal site search logs reveal what users cannot find through navigation. We will categorize the top 100 queries by intent (program research, admissions process, financial aid, campus logistics) and map each to a content or IA solution.
- **Competitive user testing:** We will test 2-3 competitor university sites (selected based on geographic and programmatic overlap) with the same task protocol, establishing a baseline for "what good looks like" in the minds of Holy Family's prospective students. ### Competitive Analysis We will analyze the digital presence of 6-8 peer and aspirational institutions selected collaboratively with your enrollment and marketing teams. The analysis covers: - Information architecture patterns (how programs are organized, how audiences are segmented)
- Conversion flow design (RFI forms, application entry points, virtual tour integration)
- Content strategy (program page depth, outcome data presentation, faculty visibility)
- Technical performance (Core Web Vitals, mobile experience, accessibility posture)
- AI search visibility (how competitor institutions appear in ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Google AI Overviews for key queries like "nursing programs in Philadelphia" or "Catholic universities near me") The output is a Competitive Landscape Matrix that identifies gaps Holy Family can exploit and standards it must meet. ### Content Strategy The content audit is not a separate workstream -- it is the foundation of the entire project. Holy Family's current 1,601 URLs include approximately 670 news articles (42% of the sitemap), 20+ duplicative request-information forms scattered across individual program pages, and 180+ top-level URL sections reflecting years of ungoverned departmental publishing. Our content strategy process: 1. **Quantitative inventory:** Every URL cataloged with page type, last modified date, traffic volume, inbound link count, and content owner.
2. **AI-assisted classification:** We use batch LLM processing to categorize each page (keep, rewrite, consolidate, archive, delete), flag duplicate content, and identify pages with no inbound links or traffic. This accelerates what would otherwise be weeks of manual triage.
3. **Content decision tree:** Each page enters a documented decision framework: - **Keep as-is** -- current, accurate, well-trafficked, structurally sound - **Rewrite** -- strategically important but poorly written, missing outcomes data, or not optimized for search - **Consolidate** -- multiple pages covering the same topic merged into a single authoritative page (e. g., the 20+ request-information forms become a single intelligent form with program routing) - **Archive** -- historically valuable but no longer current (news articles older than 2 years, past event pages, expired campaign content) - **Delete** -- test pages, seasonal greetings, duplicate URLs, broken content
4. **Content model definition:** Structured templates for each content type (program pages, faculty profiles, news articles, events, policies) with required fields, optional fields, and editorial guidelines. This is how consistency survives staff turnover.
5. **Content governance plan:** Ownership assignments, update cadences, approval workflows, and archival triggers for every content type. This is the deliverable that prevents Holy Family from returning to 1,600 ungoverned pages within three years. --- ## B.3 Information Architecture Approach ### The Multi-Audience Challenge Holy Family's current site attempts to serve every audience from every page, resulting in navigation that serves no audience well. The RFP identifies this directly: "overly complex, users can't find things." Our IA approach solves this through audience-aware pathways layered on top of a clean content hierarchy, rather than building separate audience silos that duplicate content. **Proposed Primary Architecture (8-12 sections replacing 180+):** | Section | Primary Audience | Content |
|---|---|---|
| `/programs/` | Prospective students | All academic programs with structured data, organized by school, level, and format |
| `/admissions/` | Prospective students | Application process, financial aid, campus visits, single smart RFI form |
| `/student-life/` | Prospective + current students | Housing, activities, campus ministry, resources |
| `/about/` | All audiences | Leadership, mission, history, news, accreditation |
| `/news/` | All audiences | Current news and Bold Ideas blog (2-year rolling archive) |
| `/athletics/` | Students + community | Sports programs, schedules, recruitment |
| `/alumni/` | Alumni + donors | Engagement, giving, reunions |
| `/resources/` | Current students + faculty | IT services, library, career services, registrar |
| `/directory/` | All audiences | Faculty and staff profiles with department filtering | **Audience entry points:** Rather than building separate "prospective student" and "current student" portals that create content duplication, we will implement audience-specific landing pages that curate pathways into the shared content structure. A prospective undergraduate arriving from a paid search campaign sees a hero focused on program discovery and campus visit scheduling. A current student arriving from a bookmarked link sees quick access to registration, the library, and student services. The underlying content is the same; the entry experience is personalized. ### IA Validation We do not ship information architecture based on internal logic alone. Our validation process includes: - **Card sorting** (open and closed) with 15-20 participants across audience segments to test whether our proposed groupings match user mental models
- **Tree testing** on the proposed navigation structure to measure task completion rates before any visual design is applied
- **First-click testing** on key tasks (find the Nursing program requirements, apply for financial aid, request information about the MBA) to validate that the primary navigation labels work
- **Comparison testing** against the current IA to establish measurable improvement benchmarks ### Navigation Design - **Primary navigation:** 6-8 top-level items with structured mega-menus that expose program categories and key conversion paths without requiring multiple clicks
- **Utility navigation:** Audience-specific quick links (Apply, Visit, Request Info for prospects; MyHFU portal, Email, Library for current students)
- **Contextual navigation:** Within program sections, persistent sidebar or breadcrumb navigation showing related programs, faculty, and next steps
- **Search:** Prominent, fast, and intelligent -- surfacing programs, people, and pages with type-ahead suggestions (detailed in our development approach)
- **Mobile navigation:** Full-featured bottom navigation with thumb-zone optimization, not a buried hamburger menu that hides the site's most important pathways --- ## B.4 Design Process ### Research-Driven UX Our design process begins with evidence, not mood boards. Before any visual design work begins, we will have completed: - Stakeholder interviews establishing brand positioning and enrollment priorities
- Usability testing on the current site identifying specific friction points
- Analytics analysis revealing actual user behavior patterns
- IA validation confirming the structural framework This means our designers are solving documented problems, not guessing at aesthetic preferences. ### Design System Philosophy Rick Mitchell has already invested in building a design system at Holy Family. We will not discard that work. Our approach is to audit the existing system, identify what translates to the new WordPress environment, and extend it into a comprehensive, documented component library that serves both the website build and ongoing content creation. The design system will include: - **Design tokens:** Colors, typography, spacing, shadows, and motion values defined as reusable variables that ensure consistency across every page and component
- **Component library:** 40-60 production-ready components (buttons, cards, forms, navigation elements, content blocks, media players, CTAs, testimonial displays, data tables, accordions, tabs, hero patterns) documented with usage guidelines, accessibility specifications, and responsive behavior
- **Page templates:** Defined layouts for each content type (program page, faculty profile, news article, landing page, event page) with required and optional component slots
- **Brand integration:** Holy Family's visual identity -- the purple and gold palette, the university seal, the Newtown and Northeast Philadelphia campus photography -- embedded as design tokens so that brand consistency is structural, not dependent on individual designer judgment
- **Figma handoff:** A complete Figma component library mirroring the production code, enabling Rick's team to design new pages using the same system we built ### From Wireframes to Production **Low-fidelity wireframes (Phase 2, week 1):** Grayscale layouts for 8-10 key page templates focused on content hierarchy, component placement, and conversion flow. No color, no imagery -- this is where we validate structure without aesthetic distraction. **High-fidelity mockups (Phase 2, weeks 2-3):** Full visual design applied to approved wireframes. Brand colors, typography, photography, and micro-interactions. We present 2 design directions for the homepage and 1 direction for interior pages, all built from the same component system. **Interactive prototypes (Phase 2, week 3):** Clickable prototypes for the 3 most critical user journeys (program discovery to RFI submission, admissions information to application start, homepage to campus visit request). These prototypes are tested with 5-8 users before development begins. **Design QA (ongoing during development):** Our designers review every implemented component against the approved mockups, flagging deviations before they compound. Design QA is not a phase -- it is a continuous checkpoint. ### Mobile-First, Not Mobile-Adapted Over 60% of prospective student traffic arrives on mobile devices. We design for the small screen first and enhance for larger viewports, rather than designing for desktop and then compressing. This means: - Touch targets meet the WCAG 2.2 minimum of 24x24 CSS pixels
- Form fields are optimized for mobile keyboards (tel input for phone numbers, email input for addresses)
- Critical conversion paths (Request Info, Apply, Visit) are reachable within one thumb tap from any page
- Image and video assets are responsive and lazy-loaded, with explicit width/height attributes to prevent layout shift
- Content is authored mobile-first: if a content block does not work on a phone screen, it does not ship --- ## B.5 Development Methodology ### Technical Standards We build on WordPress using modern development practices that produce maintainable, performant, secure code: - **WordPress Coding Standards** (WPCS) enforced via PHPCS linting in the CI pipeline -- every committed file passes automated style and security checks
- **Semantic HTML5** as the structural foundation -- proper heading hierarchy, landmark regions, native form elements, and ARIA attributes only where native semantics are insufficient
- **CSS architecture** using a utility-first methodology (Tailwind or custom design tokens compiled to utility classes) with BEM naming for custom components -- no specificity wars, no `!important` overrides
- **JavaScript** kept minimal and progressive -- core functionality works without JS; enhanced interactions (mega-menu animations, form validation, lazy loading) layer on top
- **PHP 8.2+** with strict typing where applicable, following WordPress Plugin API conventions for all custom functionality
- **Git-based workflow** on Pantheon: feature branches, pull requests with mandatory code review, automated testing on merge, promotion through Dev/Test/Live environments ### Quality Assurance QA is not a phase at the end of the project. It is embedded in every sprint: - **Automated testing pipeline:** - PHP linting (PHPCS with WordPress-Extra ruleset) - Accessibility linting (axe-core integrated into CI -- every pull request is scanned) - Visual regression testing (Percy or BackstopJS comparing screenshots across browsers and viewports) - Performance budgets (Lighthouse CI enforcing thresholds: Performance > 90, Accessibility > 95, Best Practices > 90) - Link checking (automated crawl on staging to catch 404s before promotion) - **Manual testing protocol:** - Cross-browser testing on Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge (current and current-1 versions) - Device testing on iOS Safari, Android Chrome, and a representative tablet viewport - Screen reader testing (VoiceOver on macOS/iOS, NVDA on Windows) for all interactive components and form flows - Keyboard-only navigation testing for every page template - **Code review process:** - Every pull request requires review by a senior developer before merge - Reviews check for: security vulnerabilities (SQL injection, XSS, CSRF), performance regressions, accessibility compliance, adherence to the component library, and code maintainability - Rick Mitchell and his team will have read access to the repository throughout development and are invited to participate in code reviews -- this is part of knowledge transfer, not a courtesy ### Performance Optimization University websites lose prospective students to slow load times. Our performance targets: | Metric | Target | Method |
|---|---|---|
| Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) | < 2.5s | Image optimization, CDN delivery, critical CSS inlining |
| First Input Delay (FID) | < 100ms | Minimal JS, deferred non-critical scripts, no render-blocking third-party tags |
| Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) | < 0.1 | Explicit image dimensions, font-display swap, stable component layouts |
| Time to First Byte (TTFB) | < 600ms | Pantheon's global CDN, Redis object caching, optimized database queries |
| Total page weight | < 1.5MB | WebP/AVIF images, code splitting, lazy loading below the fold | These targets are enforced in CI. A pull request that degrades Lighthouse performance scores below threshold cannot merge. --- ## B.6 Content Strategy & Migration ### Migration at Scale Migrating 1,601 URLs from Drupal to WordPress is not a manual copy-paste exercise. It is a structured, tool-assisted process with human quality gates at every stage. **Migration toolchain:** 1. **FG Drupal to WordPress Premium** -- reads the Drupal database directly and migrates articles, pages, taxonomies, media, and URL structures into WordPress. Handles the bulk transfer of content nodes, preserving metadata and relationships.
2. **WP All Import** -- handles refinement passes, mapping migrated content into WordPress Custom Post Types (Programs, Faculty, Events) with proper ACF field assignments.
3. **AI-assisted content processing** -- batch LLM processing to clean legacy HTML, generate missing meta descriptions, write alt text for untagged images, normalize formatting inconsistencies, and flag content that needs human rewriting. This is not AI generating content for Holy Family. It is AI accelerating the mechanical cleanup work so our writers focus on strategic content.
4. **Custom migration scripts** -- for the 5-10% of content that resists automated tooling (deeply nested Drupal Paragraphs, complex entity references, embedded media relationships).
5. **WP-CLI + Terminus** -- command-line tools for scripted batch operations on the Pantheon WordPress environment. **Migration sequence:** - Phase 1: Content audit and consolidation decisions (what moves, what merges, what archives)
- Phase 2: Content model build (WordPress CPTs, ACF field groups, taxonomies)
- Phase 3: Bulk automated migration (FG Drupal + WP All Import)
- Phase 4: AI-assisted cleanup pass (HTML normalization, metadata generation, formatting)
- Phase 5: Manual quality review (human editors verify every migrated program page, admissions page, and top-50 traffic page)
- Phase 6: Redirect map validation (every old URL tested against the new URL) ### Content Audit Methodology Our audit will classify every page in the current sitemap: | Classification | Criteria | Estimated Count |
|---|---|---|
| **Migrate and optimize** | High-traffic, enrollment-critical, or strategically important | ~400 pages |
| **Migrate as-is** | Current, accurate, adequate content | ~400 pages |
| **Consolidate** | Multiple pages on same topic merged into one (especially the 20+ standalone RFI forms) | ~200 pages reduced to ~50 |
| **Archive** | News older than 2 years, past events, expired campaigns | ~400 pages |
| **Delete** | Test pages, seasonal content, broken/empty pages | ~100 pages | The result: approximately 850-1,000 active pages in the new site, down from 1,601 -- a leaner, faster, more maintainable website that serves users better because there is less noise between them and the content they need. ### SEO, AEO, and GEO Optimization Traditional SEO is necessary but no longer sufficient. Half of prospective students now use AI tools weekly to research programs. 93% of AI search sessions end without a click to a website -- the content AI surfaces from your site IS the first impression a prospective student receives. When AI users do click through, they convert at 4.4x the rate of traditional organic visitors. Holy Family must be visible and authoritative in both traditional search and AI-mediated discovery. **Search Engine Optimization (SEO):** - Technical SEO foundation: clean URL structure, XML sitemaps, canonical tags, structured internal linking, optimized robots. txt
- On-page optimization for every program page: unique title tags, meta descriptions, heading hierarchy, image alt text
- Content depth: program pages that function as the definitive answer to "What is the [X] program at Holy Family University?" -- including enrollment numbers, graduation rates, employment outcomes with salary data, tuition per credit, student-faculty ratio, accreditations, and named student testimonials
- Validated 301 redirect map covering every active URL, generated via semantic similarity matching (embedding-based automated mapping with human review for matches below 0.98 confidence) and tested pre-launch **AI Engine Optimization (AEO):** - **Structured data (Schema. org)** on every page type: - `EducationalOrganization` on the homepage - `EducationalOccupationalProgram` on every program page (name, provider, cost, duration, occupational category, salary upon completion) - `FAQPage` on admissions, financial aid, and student life pages -- FAQ schema has the highest citation probability in AI-generated responses - `Event` for open houses and recruitment events - `Article` and `BlogPosting` on news content
- **Content architecture for citability:** Pages updated within 2 months receive significantly more AI citations than stale content. Content that includes specific statistics, named sources, and direct quotes earns 30-40% higher AI visibility. We will structure program pages to be data-rich and regularly refreshable.
- **Reading level optimization:** AI engines preferentially cite content written at a grade 6-8 reading level. Our content guidelines will specify clear, direct prose without academic jargon on public-facing pages.
- **`llms. txt` protocol:** We will place a machine-readable Markdown file at the domain root (`/llms. txt`) listing Holy Family's key institutional pages -- program directories, admissions information, tuition and financial aid facts, application deadlines. This is a low-effort, high-signal implementation that demonstrates technical sophistication. **Generative Engine Optimization (GEO):** - Monitor Holy Family's appearance in ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews, and Bing Copilot for key enrollment queries
- Establish canonical fact pages for tuition, deadlines, admission requirements, and program outcomes -- single authoritative sources that reduce the risk of AI engines synthesizing conflicting information from multiple Holy Family URLs
- Monthly content freshness updates on high-priority pages to maintain citation recency signals ### URL Structure The new URL structure will be clean, hierarchical, and permanent: Every current URL will have a corresponding 301 redirect. We will deliver a validated redirect map covering all 1,601 URLs as a project deliverable, tested both programmatically (automated crawl comparison) and manually (spot-checking the top 100 traffic pages and all externally linked pages). --- ## B.7 Stakeholder Management ### The Core Challenge University websites involve more content stakeholders than almost any other type of project. At Holy Family, four academic Schools, enrollment management, student affairs, campus ministry, athletics, advancement, IT, the president's office, and marketing all have legitimate content needs and strong opinions about how the website should represent their work. Left unmanaged, this produces scope creep, design-by-committee, and a website that tries to say everything and communicates nothing. ### Our Framework: Structured Input, Clear Authority **Steering Committee (5-7 members)**
Meets biweekly. Includes the VP for Marketing and Communications, Director of Digital Experience, VP for Enrollment Management, a representative academic dean (rotating), and VP for Institutional Effectiveness. This group makes binding decisions on scope, timeline, design direction, and content priorities. Decisions documented in writing within 24 hours. **Content Working Groups (by School/Division)**
Each of the four Schools and each major administrative division (enrollment, student life, advancement, campus ministry) designates a single content liaison. Content liaisons attend monthly working sessions where they review content templates, provide department-specific content, and validate program information accuracy. They do not attend design reviews or make architectural decisions. **Decision Escalation Protocol**
When two stakeholders disagree, the disagreement is documented with both positions and escalated to the Steering Committee at the next meeting (or within 48 hours if blocking). The Steering Committee decides. This protocol is established in writing at project kickoff and referenced throughout -- it prevents hallway conversations from overriding structured decisions. **Feedback Windows**
Each design and content milestone has a defined feedback window (typically 5 business days). Feedback submitted within the window is incorporated. Feedback submitted after the window is documented for a future phase. This prevents the project from entering an infinite revision loop while respecting every stakeholder's right to be heard. ### Consensus Building We have found that stakeholder conflict in higher education usually stems from fear of invisibility -- departments worry that the redesign will bury their content. We address this proactively: - **Content visibility audit:** Early in discovery, we show each department where their content currently lives, how much traffic it receives, and where it will live in the new IA. This replaces anxiety with data.
- **Template demonstrations:** Before asking departments to commit content to templates, we show populated examples using their actual content. Seeing "this is what your program page will look like" resolves more objections than any amount of wireframe explanation.
- **Shared metrics dashboard:** Post-launch, every content owner sees how their pages perform (views, engagement, conversion) through a shared GA4 dashboard. This shifts the conversation from "is my content visible?" to "is my content working?" --- ## B.8 Collaborative Development Process ### Working with Rick Mitchell's Team The RFP makes clear that Holy Family has a technically capable internal digital team led by Rick Mitchell, a practitioner with UX engineering experience at EY, Apple Leisure Group, and Urban Outfitters, and a Cornell UX Design credential. This is not a client who needs to be educated about web development. This is a client who needs a partner that respects existing institutional knowledge and extends the team's capacity rather than replacing it. Our collaboration model: **Shared Repository Access**
Rick's team will have full read access to the project repository from day one. They will see every commit, every pull request, every code review comment. They are invited (but not required) to participate in code reviews, ask questions, and flag concerns. Every custom component, every ACF field group, every custom query, and every integration point is documented in a project wiki accessible to Rick's team. Documentation is not an afterthought delivered at the end of the project. It is written alongside the code, reviewed alongside the code, and delivered as a living reference. ### Knowledge Transfer Program Knowledge transfer is not a half-day training session at the end of the project. All training materials become Holy Family's permanent property. --- ## B.9 Risk Management ### Identified Risks and Mitigation Strategies | Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation | Contingency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Content delivery delays from departments** | High | High -- blocks migration and testing | Content liaisons assigned per department at kickoff; content templates with required fields delivered in Week 4; biweekly content deadline reminders with Steering Committee escalation for non-response | Placeholder content in staging allows development to continue; delayed content enters post-launch optimization queue |
| **Drupal Paragraphs complexity** | Medium | High -- can slow migration by 2-4 weeks | Pre-migration Drupal audit to catalog all Paragraph types and nesting patterns; Gutenberg block mapping decided before migration begins, not during | Reserve 80 hours in the migration budget for manual Paragraph conversion if automated tooling fails on deeply nested structures |
| **Scope creep from stakeholder requests** | High | Medium -- erodes timeline and budget | Structured feedback windows; Steering Committee authority; documented scope with change order process for additions | Phase 2 backlog absorbs legitimate requests that surface after design approval without disrupting the launch timeline |

Not headless WordPress. Not a decoupled architecture. Traditional, full-stack WordPress -- the same platform that powers 43% of the web and the majority of higher education institutions that have migrated away from Drupal in the past five years. This recommendation is grounded in three factors specific to Holy Family's situation. ### Why WordPress Is the Right Fit for a 3,700-Student University **1. Editorial usability for a small team.**
Rick Mitchell's team maintains the current site with one to two staff members. WordPress's native editing experience -- Gutenberg block editor, real-time preview, in-context editing, media library management -- is purpose-built for teams that need to publish and update content without developer intervention. Drupal's editorial tools have improved, but WordPress remains the benchmark for content author experience. When your web team is measured in single digits, the CMS must work for them, not against them. **2. The plugin and developer ecosystem is unmatched.**
WordPress has the largest active developer community of any CMS. This matters for a practical reason: when Holy Family needs to hire a developer, find a freelancer for a specific integration, or troubleshoot an issue, WordPress talent is abundant and competitively priced. Drupal's talent pool is smaller and more expensive. Over a five-year ownership horizon, this difference compounds. **3. Lower total cost of ownership.**
WordPress core is free. The premium tools required for this project -- ACF Pro, Gravity Forms, a managed hosting plan on Pantheon -- total under $1,000 per year in licensing costs. Drupal's module ecosystem is also free, but the development and maintenance labor required to achieve equivalent functionality is consistently higher. For a university operating with fiscal discipline, WordPress delivers more capability per dollar spent. ### Why Not Headless WordPress Headless architecture -- where WordPress serves only as a content API and a separate JavaScript application (React, Next. js) renders the front end -- is a legitimate approach for some institutions. It is not the right approach here. **Editorial workflow degrades.** In a headless setup, the content preview, in-context editing, and WYSIWYG experience that makes WordPress intuitive all require custom re-engineering. Your team would lose the ability to see what a page looks like before publishing it unless we build that capability from scratch. **The plugin ecosystem breaks.** Gravity Forms, Yoast SEO, accessibility checking tools, Google Tag Manager integration -- all of these work out of the box in traditional WordPress. In a headless architecture, each one requires front-end reimplementation or replacement. **It creates a permanent skills gap.** A headless front end requires React or Next. js expertise for ongoing maintenance. If your internal team's strengths are in WordPress theming, PHP, and content management, headless introduces a technology your team cannot maintain independently. **Two codebases instead of one.** Headless means maintaining the WordPress backend and a separate front-end application. For a site that needs to be stable and maintainable for five or more years, this doubles the surface area for bugs, security updates, and technical debt. ### Future-Proofing Without Headless We will architect the content model using Advanced Custom Fields (ACF) Pro with full REST API exposure. Every custom post type, every field group, every taxonomy will be accessible via the WordPress REST API from day one. If Holy Family ever needs to serve content to a mobile app, a digital signage system, or a future front-end framework, the content layer is ready. You get the editorial experience of traditional WordPress today and the API flexibility of headless architecture if you ever need it. --- ## C.2 Technical Architecture ### Proposed Technology Stack | Layer | Technology | Purpose |
