Pantheon provides a managed WordPress hosting environment built around a three-environment workflow that enforces development best practices: **Development (Dev)**
The working environment where code changes are made. Developers write and test theme and plugin updates here. The Dev environment connects directly to a Git repository -- every code change is committed, tracked, and reversible. **Test**
A staging environment that mirrors the Live site's content and database. Code is promoted from Dev to Test for review. This is where the Holy Family team will preview changes, run accessibility audits, and approve updates before they reach the public site. **Live**
The production environment serving holyfamily. edu. Code is promoted from Test to Live only after approval. The Live environment sits behind Pantheon's Global CDN (powered by Fastly), providing edge caching, automatic HTTPS, and DDoS protection. ### How Content and Code Move Pantheon enforces a deliberate separation: - **Code moves up:** Dev to Test to Live. Every promotion is a Git-based deployment with a full audit trail.
- **Content moves down:** Database and files can be cloned from Live to Dev or Test for local development and testing. This means developers always work against real content, not placeholder data. This workflow eliminates the most common source of deployment failures in WordPress: ad hoc code changes made directly on production. On Pantheon, the Live filesystem is read-only. Code changes can only reach production through the Dev-to-Test-to-Live pipeline. ### Version Control and Deployment Process All custom theme code, custom plugin code, and configuration files are stored in a Git repository hosted on Pantheon. The deployment process: 1. Developer creates a feature branch (or uses a Multidev environment -- see Section C.6)
2. Code is written, tested locally, and pushed to the branch
3. Changes are reviewed (code review by a second developer when team size permits)
4. Branch is merged to the Dev environment's main branch
5. Code is promoted to Test, where it is reviewed against the production database
6. After approval, code is promoted to Live
7. Pantheon executes cache clears automatically on deployment This process is fully auditable. Every change to the codebase has a commit message, a timestamp, and an author. If a deployment introduces a problem, Pantheon supports one-click rollback to any previous commit. ### Uptime and Performance Pantheon's infrastructure delivers: - **99.9% uptime SLA** on Performance plans and above
- **Global CDN** with edge nodes across North America, Europe, and Asia-Pacific
- **Automated HTTPS** via Let's Encrypt, including certificate renewal
- **Container-based architecture** that scales automatically during traffic spikes (enrollment season, admissions deadlines, event announcements)
- **Daily automated backups** with one-click restore --- ## C.4 Accessibility Compliance: WCAG 2.2 AA ### The Compliance Landscape The ADA Title II Final Rule (published April 2024) required public universities to meet WCAG 2.1 AA by April 24, 2024. That deadline has passed. As a recipient of federal funds, Holy Family University is subject to Section 504 and Section 508 requirements that impose the same standard. We will build to WCAG 2.2 AA -- one version ahead of the current legal floor. WCAG 2.2 is backward-compatible with 2.1, so meeting 2.2 automatically satisfies the legal requirement while providing a compliance margin against future regulatory updates. ### What WCAG 2.2 AA Adds WCAG 2.2 introduces six new success criteria beyond 2.1. Four are directly relevant to a university website: | Criterion | Requirement | How We Address It |
|---|---|---|
| **2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured** | Keyboard focus indicator must not be hidden behind sticky headers, modals, or other overlapping elements | Focus management designed into every component; sticky header accounts for focus offset |
| **2.5.7 Dragging Movements** | Any drag interaction must have a click or tap alternative | No drag-only interactions in the design; all interactive elements have standard click/tap paths |
| **2.5.8 Target Size Minimum** | Interactive targets must be at least 24x24 CSS pixels | Minimum touch target of 44x44px in our component library (exceeds the 24px floor) |
| **3.3.8 Accessible Authentication** | Login cannot require cognitive function tests (e. g., CAPTCHA puzzles); must allow password managers and paste | All forms support autofill and paste; CAPTCHA alternatives (honeypot, reCAPTCHA v3 score-based) used where spam protection is needed | ### "Shift Left" Accessibility Methodology Most agencies treat accessibility as a QA checkpoint -- build the site first, then audit and fix. This is expensive, slow, and produces inconsistent results. Our approach shifts accessibility left into every phase of the project: **Design Phase**
- Color palettes validated for 4.5:1 contrast ratio (body text) and 3:1 (large text and interactive elements) before a single pixel is designed
- Touch targets sized at 44x44px minimum in wireframes, not retrofitted later
- Focus states designed as a first-class visual element for every interactive component
- Heading hierarchy defined in content models, enforcing logical structure before content is written **Development Phase**
- Component library built with semantic HTML and ARIA attributes from the first line of code
- Automated accessibility linting integrated into the development environment (axe-core) -- developers see violations as they write code, not weeks later in a QA report
- axe-core integrated into the CI/CD pipeline -- code that introduces accessibility regressions cannot be deployed **Content Phase**
- WordPress editor configured with guardrails: alt text required before image insertion, heading level enforcement (no skipping from H2 to H4), accessible PDF templates provided for document uploads
- Content author training includes hands-on accessibility exercises, not just a policy document **QA Phase**
- Automated testing via axe-core catches approximately 30-40% of WCAG violations
- Manual testing covers the rest: keyboard-only navigation through all critical user flows, screen reader testing with VoiceOver and NVDA on key pages (homepage, program pages, admissions application, financial aid)
- Accessibility testing is not a separate phase -- it runs in parallel with functional QA throughout development **Post-Launch**
- Holy Family already uses Siteimprove for accessibility monitoring. We will configure Siteimprove dashboards for ongoing WCAG 2.2 AA tracking, providing the internal team with continuous visibility into compliance status
- Quarterly audit cadence recommended for the first year post-launch ### Common University Website Failures We Will Prevent Based on industry data, the most frequently failed WCAG criteria on university websites are: - Missing image alt text (1.1.1) -- enforced at the editor level
- Insufficient color contrast (1.4.3) -- validated in the design system
- Keyboard navigation failures (2.1.1) -- tested for every interactive component
- Missing video captions (1.2.2) -- captioning requirements documented in content guidelines; Wistia (already in your stack) supports captions natively
- Inaccessible PDFs (1.3.1) -- accessible PDF templates provided; legacy PDF remediation scoped as a post-launch phase
- Form labeling failures (3.3.2) -- all Gravity Forms fields include programmatic labels, error messages, and instructions --- ## C.5 Integration Capabilities ### Slate CRM Integration Slate is Holy Family's enrollment CRM. A successful website redesign must integrate cleanly with Slate at three levels: form submissions, behavioral tracking, and content syndication. #### Form Integration: Gravity Forms Webhook Pattern We recommend the Gravity Forms webhook approach over Slate's native script-tag embed. Here is why. **The Slate embed approach** requires pasting Slate's `<script>` tag into WordPress pages. This creates three problems: WordPress's content sanitizer can strip or break the script tag (requiring a custom block or shortcode workaround), the injected form inherits Slate's CSS which can conflict with the site's design system, and you are limited to one Slate form per page. **The Gravity Forms webhook approach** solves all three: 1. **Build the form natively in WordPress.** Using Gravity Forms, we create inquiry forms (Request for Information, Schedule a Visit, Apply Now) that match the site's design system exactly. The forms use the same typography, colors, spacing, and validation patterns as every other component on the site. 2. **Map fields to Slate's Source Format.** Each Gravity Forms field is mapped to the corresponding Slate field key (first name, last name, email, phone, program of interest, etc). This mapping is configured once per form. 3. **POST data to Slate on submission.** The Gravity Forms Webhooks Add-On sends form data as a JSON payload to Slate's Source Format endpoint in real time. When a prospective student submits an RFI form, the data arrives in Slate within seconds -- not in a batch overnight. 4. **Confirmation and tracking.** After submission, the student sees a WordPress-native confirmation page (or inline confirmation) while Slate simultaneously creates or updates their prospect record and triggers any configured Slate workflows (automated emails, counselor assignments, drip campaigns). This pattern has been documented and validated at scale by NC State University, including complete field key mappings between Gravity Forms and Slate. #### Behavioral Tracking: Slate Ping Slate Ping is a lightweight JavaScript snippet that tracks page-level browsing behavior across the website. It will be implemented site-wide via the theme's header template (or via Google Tag Manager, which Holy Family already uses): Slate Ping drops a first-party cookie and records every page a visitor views. When that anonymous visitor later performs an authenticated action -- clicking a link in a Slate email, logging into the applicant portal, submitting a form -- Slate links their browsing history to their prospect record retroactively. This provides the enrollment team with a complete picture of a student's research journey: which programs they explored, which financial aid pages they read, how many times they returned before converting. This is the behavioral tracking foundation for enrollment attribution. It will be active on every page from launch day. #### Content Syndication: Slate Web Services For dynamic content that originates in Slate -- program listings, event calendars, deadline dates -- we will use Slate's Query Builder web service to pull structured JSON data into WordPress. This enables scenarios such as: - Displaying upcoming admissions events on the website, sourced from Slate's event calendar
- Showing real-time application deadlines that update when the enrollment team changes them in Slate
- Populating "Programs of Interest" dropdowns on forms directly from Slate's program taxonomy The web service approach ensures the website always reflects current enrollment data without manual content duplication. #### Integration Architecture Summary | Data Flow | Method | Timing |
|---|---|---|
| Form submissions (website to Slate) | Gravity Forms webhook to Slate Source Format endpoint | Real-time on submission |
| Browsing behavior (website to Slate) | Slate Ping JavaScript snippet on every page | Continuous; linked to records nightly |
| Dynamic content (Slate to website) | Slate Query Builder web service (JSON) | Cached with configurable refresh interval |
| Applicant portal (Slate-hosted) | Embedded iframe or linked portal page | Requires SSO configuration with IT (SAML/CAS) | #### Technical Dependencies and Boundaries - Slate Knowledge Base access is required for implementation. We will need credentials to Holy Family's Slate instance to configure Source Format endpoints and Query Builder web services.
- The applicant status portal requires cross-domain single sign-on (SAML, CAS, or LDAP). This is an IT infrastructure dependency, not a web development deliverable. We will coordinate with IT but scope SSO configuration as a separate workstream.
- Slate's Open API (launched read-only in 2023) may provide additional integration options. We will evaluate during discovery. ### Additional Integration Points | System | Integration Method | Notes |

Routine decisions (resolved by project team)**
Examples: CSS implementation details, plugin selection within approved categories, content formatting conventions.
Process: The team decides and documents in the weekly status update. No HFU approval required. **Tier 2. Design and content decisions (HFU stakeholder input)**
Examples: Navigation structure, page layout choices, content consolidation recommendations, form field definitions, homepage content hierarchy.
Process: We present a recommendation with rationale. HFU provides feedback within 3 business days. If no response within 3 business days, we proceed with our recommendation (documented in writing). **Tier 3. Strategic and contractual decisions (executive approval)**
Examples: Scope changes, budget adjustments, timeline modifications, CMS architecture changes, integration additions, launch date changes.
Process: Written proposal with impact analysis (scope, timeline, budget). Joint meeting with Rick Mitchell and Katherine Primus (or designees). Formal written approval required before work proceeds. ### Approval Gates Five formal approval gates. No phase begins until the preceding gate is cleared: | Gate | Timing | What Is Approved | Who Approves |
|---|---|---|---|
| G1: Discovery complete | End of Phase 1 | Content model, IA, technical approach | Rick Mitchell + Katherine Primus |
| G2: Design approved | End of Phase 2 | Visual design, component library, responsive layouts | Rick Mitchell + Katherine Primus |
| G3: Development complete | End of Phase 3 | Feature-complete site (functionality, not content) | Rick Mitchell |
| G4: Content and testing complete | End of Phase 4 | Migrated content, QA results, WCAG conformance | Rick Mitchell + Katherine Primus |
| G5: Launch authorization | End of Phase 5 | UAT sign-off, training complete, launch readiness | Katherine Primus (or executive designee) | ### Stakeholder Management A project of this scope touches many content owners. Admissions, academics, student life, athletics, alumni, IT, and executive leadership. Our approach: 1. **Stakeholder matrix defined in Week 1.** We map every stakeholder to a role: decision-maker, contributor, reviewer, or informed. This prevents scope creep from well-meaning contributors who believe they are decision-makers.
2. **Content owner workshops.** During Phase 1, we conduct two group workshops with content owners to gather requirements. This replaces the slower approach of 15+ individual interviews.
3. **Feedback consolidation.** Rick Mitchell serves as the single point of consolidation for HFU feedback. Conflicting direction from multiple stakeholders is reconciled before it reaches the project team.
4. **Decision log.** Every Tier 2 and Tier 3 decision is logged with date, decision, rationale, and approver. This prevents revisiting settled decisions later in the project. --- ## D.3 Change Management ### Scope Change Process Website projects of this complexity will encounter scope changes. The goal is not to prevent all changes but to ensure every change is intentional, documented, and understood in terms of its impact. **Step 1: Change request submitted.**
Either party submits a change request (CR) describing the desired change. The CR must include: what is changing, why it is needed, and who is requesting it. **Step 2: Impact assessment (2 business days).**
We assess the CR against three dimensions: | Dimension | Assessment |
|---|---|
| **Scope** | What work is added, removed, or modified? Which deliverables are affected? |
| **Timeline** | Does this change the critical path? By how many days/weeks? Which milestones shift? |
| **Budget** | What is the cost impact? Is this within existing scope or does it require additional budget? | We deliver the impact assessment in writing, including our recommendation (approve, defer to post-launch, or decline with alternative). **Step 3: Approval.**
- CRs with no budget or timeline impact: approved by Rick Mitchell.
- CRs with timeline impact of 3 business days or less: approved by Rick Mitchell.
- CRs with budget impact or timeline impact greater than 3 business days: approved by Rick Mitchell and Katherine Primus. **Step 4: Documentation and execution.**
Approved CRs are logged in the project record, the project plan is updated, and any affected milestones are revised. The weekly status meeting reflects the change. ### Common Scope Changes in Higher Ed Redesigns Based on prior engagements, the following changes frequently emerge mid-project. We flag them so they can be discussed during Discovery rather than surface as surprises: - **Additional integrations** discovered during technical discovery (LMS, SIS, course catalog feed, campus maps)
- **New content types** requested by stakeholders not involved in initial discovery (alumni stories, research spotlights, podcast pages)
- **Expanded accessibility scope** (remediation of legacy PDF documents, video captioning backlog)
- **Additional language support** (Spanish-language pages for bilingual recruitment)
- **Enhanced personalization** beyond base scope (funnel-stage content, geo-targeted messaging) We will proactively identify these possibilities during Phase 1 and provide cost estimates so HFU can make informed decisions before development begins. --- ## D.4 Quality Assurance ### QA Philosophy Quality is not a phase. Testing follows a three-layer approach: **Layer 1: Automated scanning (catches ~30. 40% of issues)**
- axe-core integrated into CI pipeline. Runs on every code commit
- Full-site axe scan of all pages post-migration
- Siteimprove continuous monitoring (HFU already uses this tool. We will configure it for the new site)
- HTML validation via W3C validator **Layer 2: Manual expert testing (catches ~40. 50% of issues)**
- Keyboard-only navigation of all primary user flows (prospective student journey, application flow, program discovery, RFI submission, faculty directory search)
- Screen reader testing using VoiceOver (macOS/iOS) and NVDA (Windows) on priority pages
- Focus order verification on all interactive components
- Color contrast verification for all text/background combinations, including within images and over gradients
- Touch target size verification (24×24 CSS pixel minimum per WCAG 2.2 criterion 2.5.8) **Layer 3: Assistive technology user testing (catches remaining ~10. 20%)**
- We recommend HFU engage 2. 3 users of assistive technology (screen reader, switch access, magnification) for UAT. We will provide test scripts and facilitate the sessions. **WCAG 2.2 AA-specific items we explicitly test:** | Criterion | What We Test |
|---|---|
| 2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured | Keyboard focus indicator is never hidden behind sticky headers, modals, or overlays |
| 2.5.7 Dragging Movements | Any drag interaction (e. g., image carousel, map) has a click/tap alternative |
| 2.5.8 Target Size Minimum | All interactive elements meet 24×24 CSS pixel minimum |
| 3.2.6 Consistent Help | Help/contact links appear in the same location on every page |
| 3.3.7 Redundant Entry | Multi-step forms auto-populate previously entered data |
| 3.3.8 Accessible Authentication | Login flows allow password managers and paste; no CAPTCHA without alternative | **Deliverable:** A WCAG 2.2 AA conformance report documenting every tested criterion, with pass/fail/partial status, evidence (screenshots, code references), and remediation details for any issues found. This report serves as the basis for an Accessibility Conformance Report (ACR) / VPAT if required by HFU's procurement process. #### User Acceptance Testing (UAT) UAT is the final validation that the site meets HFU's requirements as experienced by real users. **UAT Structure:** | Element | Detail |

That preference is well-founded: for a two-person internal web team managing a site over a five-year horizon, WordPress offers the largest talent pool, the most mature plugin ecosystem, and the lowest total cost of ownership of any enterprise CMS. We build on WordPress and deploy to Pantheon as our standard for higher education engagements. Drupal-to-WordPress migration is a practiced methodology for the team, not an exploratory project. ### AI-Accelerated Content Migration We do not migrate content by hand at $X per hour. We use an AI-accelerated methodology that delivers higher quality at lower cost across five specific stages: **Content Audit.** Before a single page moves, we classify all 1,601 URLs using a large language model batch process. The model identifies duplicates, flags outdated content, clusters pages by topic similarity, and recommends consolidation candidates. For Holy Family, our preliminary analysis of the live sitemap has already identified approximately 670 news articles (42% of the site), 20+ redundant request-information forms, 180+ distinct top-level URL sections, and 20-30 pages of test and seasonal cruft. This is not a 1,601-page migration. It is a ~900. 1,000-page migration with a documented archival strategy for the rest, and that distinction saves real money. **Semantic Redirect Mapping.** Most agencies build redirect maps in a spreadsheet, matching old URLs to new ones by hand. We crawl both the source and destination sites, generate vector embeddings for every page using sentence-transformer models, and auto-accept matches above a 0.98 similarity threshold. Matches below that threshold get human review. The result is a validated 301 redirect map for every active URL on launch day. Not a best-effort spreadsheet delivered the week before go-live. **Schema Mapping.** The AI analyzes Drupal's field configuration and generates a first-draft WordPress content model (custom post types, ACF field groups, taxonomy definitions) as structured JSON. A senior developer reviews and refines. This eliminates the tedious manual translation step and ensures nothing is lost between platforms. **Content Transformation.** Legacy Drupal HTML is cleaned, monolithic body fields are split into structured components, missing meta descriptions and alt text are generated, and formatting is normalized. All through batch LLM processing with human validation. **Post-Migration QA.** An automated comparison engine checks every migrated page against its source: empty fields, broken references, formatting drift, missing media. Issues surface as a prioritized spreadsheet, not a manual spot-check weeks after launch. The governing principle: AI drafts, humans decide, code executes, tests verify. No content goes live without human review. The AI accelerates the work that is slow and repetitive; it does not replace the judgment that matters. ### Content Consolidation Strategy Your sitemap contains 1,601 URLs, but the active, enrollment-relevant content is closer to 900. 1,000 pages. The difference is six years of accumulated news articles that consume crawl budget without driving enrollment, duplicate program landing pages that fragment analytics, 20+ standalone request-information forms that should be a single smart form with CRM routing, and seasonal or anniversary content that has passed its useful life. We propose reducing the active page count from 1,601 to approximately 900. 1,000 pages during migration. This is not cutting corners. It is content strategy. Fewer pages means a faster migration, a tighter information architecture, stronger SEO signal concentration on the pages that matter, and a site that Rick Mitchell's team can actually govern over the long term. | Inquiry-to-application | 15-25% | 30-50% |
| Bounce rate | 55% | 40% or lower | We build these outcomes into the project through basic personalization in Year 1 (geo-targeting for in-state vs. out-of-state messaging, UTM-based hero content variation, conditional CTAs based on visit depth), A/B testing frameworks on key conversion pages, and a post-launch optimization roadmap that sustains gains beyond the initial redesign lift. Ferris State achieved a homepage click-through rate increase from 1.6% to 14% with geo-targeting alone. Harper College saw a 962% increase in applications using funnel-stage personalization. These are not aspirational. They are documented outcomes from comparable institutions using the same tools we deploy. ### WCAG 2.2 AA Accessibility The ADA Title II final rule required public-facing university websites to meet WCAG 2.1 AA by April 24, 2024. That deadline has passed. Any institution that has not begun compliance work is exposed. We build to WCAG 2.2 AA. Which exceeds the legal floor, provides compliance margin, and is fully backward-compatible with 2.1. WCAG 2.2 adds six new success criteria beyond 2.1, including focus visibility requirements for sticky headers and modals, minimum 24x24 CSS pixel touch targets, accessible authentication without cognitive tests, and redundant entry protections. Our "shift left" accessibility process bakes compliance into every phase: - **Design phase:** Accessible color palettes verified at 4.5:1 contrast, 24px touch targets in wireframes, focus states designed for every interactive element, logical heading hierarchy in content models.
- **Development phase:** Accessible component library with ARIA built in, semantic HTML enforced by default, automated axe-core linting in the CI pipeline.
- **Content phase:** WYSIWYG editor guardrails requiring alt text before publish, heading level enforcement, accessible PDF templates.
- **QA phase:** Automated CI testing plus manual screen reader testing (VoiceOver, NVDA) on every key user flow.
- **Post-launch:** Continuous monitoring via Siteimprove (which Holy Family already uses) with quarterly manual audits. No automated tool catches more than 30-40% of WCAG violations. We do not rely solely on automated scans. Every critical user flow. Program discovery, RFI submission, application start, campus visit registration. Receives manual keyboard navigation and screen reader testing before launch. ### Pantheon as the Higher Education Standard Pantheon's Dev/Test/Live architecture, Git-based code deployment, and Autopilot automated update system are the workflow standard for WordPress in higher education. We deploy to Pantheon specifically because it enforces the discipline that keeps a university site stable over a five-year lifecycle: - **Code moves up, content moves down.** Development changes flow through Dev to Test to Live. Content and configuration flow from Live back to Dev. This prevents the "works on my machine" failures that plague less structured hosting.
- **Multidev environments** allow parallel development and content-type testing without risking the production site.
- **Autopilot** runs plugin and core updates automatically with visual regression testing before promotion. Reducing the maintenance burden on Rick Mitchell's team.
- **Read-only filesystem in production** eliminates an entire class of security vulnerabilities. No plugin can write to the production codebase; all code deploys through Git. If Holy Family is already on Pantheon for Drupal (as is likely for a university of this profile), the WordPress migration is simpler: same infrastructure, same workflow, new CMS. --- ## G.3 How This Engagement Fits Holy Family Specifically Rather than compare ourselves to other respondents, we want to be specific about why this engagement model fits Holy Family's actual situation. ### Sized for the institution, not for an enterprise budget Higher-ed agency benchmarks for projects of this scope frequently land between $250,000 and $450,000+ (est., based on published RFP awards at peer agencies such as OHO Interactive and Kanopi Studios).
